subject: physics
title: SUVAT: Find Final Velocity
stem_template: An object starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at \\({{a}}\\) m s\\(^{-2}\\) for \\({{t}}\\) s. Calculate the final velocity of the object.

subject: physics
title: SUVAT: Find Displacement
stem_template: A car travelling at \\({{u}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) decelerates uniformly at \\({{a}}\\) m s\\(^{-2}\\) until it stops. Calculate the distance travelled before stopping.

subject: physics
title: Projectile Maximum Height
stem_template: A ball is thrown vertically upward with initial speed \\({{u}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\). What is the maximum height reached? (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))

subject: physics
title: Projectile Motion: Range and Time of Flight
stem_template: A projectile is launched from ground level at \\({{v0}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) at an angle of \\({{theta}}°\\) above the horizontal. (a) Find the time of flight. (b) Find the horizontal range. (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))
mark_scheme_template: Vertical component: \\(u_y = {{v0}}\\sin{{theta}}°\\). Time of flight: \\(T = \\frac{2u_y}{g}\\). Horizontal range: \\(R = v_0 \\cos{{theta}}° \\times T\\) [4 marks]
hints: Split velocity into horizontal and vertical components
Vertical motion: use s = ut + ½at²

subject: physics
title: Velocity-Time Graph Interpretation
stem_template: A velocity-time graph shows a straight line from \\((0, {{u}})\\) to \\(({{t}}, {{v}})\\). What is the acceleration?
mark_scheme_template: Acceleration = gradient = \\(\\frac{\\Delta v}{\\Delta t} = \\frac{{{v}} - {{u}}}{{{t}}} = {{(v-u)/t}}\\) m s\\(^{-2}\\) [1 mark]

subject: physics
title: Newton's Second Law: Net Force
stem_template: A mass of \\({{m}}\\) kg accelerates at \\({{a}}\\) m s\\(^{-2}\\) on a frictionless surface. Calculate the net force acting on it.

subject: physics
title: Impulse and Change in Momentum
stem_template: A \\({{m}}\\) kg ball moving at \\({{u}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) is brought to rest in \\({{t}}\\) ms. Calculate the average force exerted on the ball.
mark_scheme_template: Impulse = change in momentum: \\(F \\Delta t = m \\Delta v\\). \\(F = \\frac{m \\Delta v}{\\Delta t} = \\frac{{{m}} \\times {{u}}}{{{t}} \\times 10^{-3}} = {{m*u/(t/1000)}}\\) N [3 marks]
hints: Convert ms to s: divide by 1000

subject: physics
title: Conservation of Momentum
stem_template: A \\({{m1}}\\) kg trolley moving at \\({{v1}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) collides and sticks to a stationary \\({{m2}}\\) kg trolley. What is their common velocity after collision?
hints: Total momentum before = total momentum after

subject: physics
title: Friction and Inclined Plane
stem_template: A block of mass \\({{m}}\\) kg rests on a plane inclined at \\({{theta}}°\\) to the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction is \\({{mu}}\\). (a) Draw a free-body diagram and label all forces. (b) Determine whether the block slides or remains stationary. (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))
mark_scheme_template: (a) Weight downward, normal force perpendicular to plane, friction up the slope. (b) Component of weight down slope = \\(mg\\sin{{theta}}°\\). Maximum static friction = \\({{mu}} mg\\cos{{theta}}°\\). If \\(\\tan{{theta}}° > {{mu}}\\), block slides. [4 marks]
hints: Resolve forces parallel and perpendicular to the slope
Compare tanθ with μ to determine if sliding occurs

subject: physics
title: Kinetic Energy Calculation
stem_template: Calculate the kinetic energy of a \\({{m}}\\) kg object moving at \\({{v}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\).

subject: physics
title: Work Done Against Gravity
stem_template: A \\({{m}}\\) kg crate is lifted vertically through \\({{h}}\\) m at constant speed. Calculate the work done against gravity. (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))
hints: Work against gravity = gain in gravitational PE

subject: physics
title: Power Output
stem_template: A motor lifts a \\({{m}}\\) kg load through \\({{h}}\\) m in \\({{t}}\\) s. What is the useful output power? (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))

subject: physics
title: Energy Conservation with Friction
stem_template: A \\({{m}}\\) kg block slides down a frictionless ramp from a height of \\({{h}}\\) m. (a) Find the speed at the bottom. (b) If there were friction and the block arrived at speed \\({{vf}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\), find the energy lost to friction. (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(v = \\sqrt{2 \\times 9.8 \\times {{h}}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) [2 marks]. (b) \\(E_{lost} = mgh - \\frac{1}{2}mv_f^2 = {{m}} \\times 9.8 \\times {{h}} - \\frac{1}{2} \\times {{m}} \\times {{vf}}^2\\) J [2 marks]
hints: Energy lost = initial PE − final KE

subject: physics
title: Torque Calculation
stem_template: A force of \\({{F}}\\) N is applied perpendicular to a wrench at a distance of \\({{r}}\\) m from the pivot. Calculate the torque produced.

subject: physics
title: Rotational Kinetic Energy
stem_template: A solid disk of mass \\({{m}}\\) kg and radius \\({{r}}\\) m rotates at angular velocity \\({{omega}}\\) rad s\\(^{-1}\\). The moment of inertia of a solid disk is \\(I = \\frac{1}{2}mr^2\\). What is the rotational kinetic energy?

subject: physics
title: Angular Momentum Conservation
stem_template: A figure skater spinning with arms outstretched has moment of inertia \\({{I1}}\\) kg m\\(^2\\) and angular velocity \\({{omega1}}\\) rad s\\(^{-1}\\). She pulls her arms in, reducing her moment of inertia to \\({{I2}}\\) kg m\\(^2\\). Calculate her new angular velocity.
mark_scheme_template: Angular momentum conserved: \\(I_1 \\omega_1 = I_2 \\omega_2\\). \\(\\omega_2 = \\frac{I_1 \\omega_1}{I_2} = \\frac{{{I1}} \\times {{omega1}}}{{{I2}}} = {{I1*omega1/I2}}\\) rad s\\(^{-1}\\) [3 marks]
hints: Angular momentum L = Iω is conserved
No external torque acts on the skater

subject: physics
title: Equilibrium of a Beam
stem_template: A uniform beam of length \\({{L}}\\) m and mass \\({{M}}\\) kg is pivoted at one end. A weight of \\({{W}}\\) N hangs from the other end. What force \\(F\\) must be applied upward at \\({{d}}\\) m from the pivot to keep it horizontal? (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))
mark_scheme_template: Take moments about pivot: \\(F \\times {{d}} = M g \\times \\frac{L}{2} + W \\times L\\). \\(F = \\frac{{{M}} \\times 9.8 \\times {{L/2}} + {{W}} \\times {{L}}}{{{d}}}\\) N [3 marks]
hints: Sum of clockwise moments = sum of anticlockwise moments
Weight of beam acts at its centre of mass

subject: physics
title: Time Dilation
stem_template: A spaceship moves at \\({{beta}}c\\) relative to Earth. A clock on the spaceship records a proper time of \\({{t0}}\\) s for an event. Calculate the time measured by an Earth observer. (c = 3 × 10\\(^8\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: Lorentz factor: \\(\\gamma = \\frac{1}{\\sqrt{1-\\beta^2}} = \\frac{1}{\\sqrt{1-{{beta}}^2}}\\). Dilated time: \\(t = \\gamma t_0 = {{t0/sqrt(1-beta*beta)}}\\) s [3 marks]
hints: Proper time is measured in the rest frame

subject: physics
title: Length Contraction
stem_template: A rod has proper length \\({{L0}}\\) m. It moves at \\({{beta}}c\\) relative to an observer. What length does the observer measure?
hints: Length contraction occurs along direction of motion only

subject: physics
title: Relativistic Mass-Energy
stem_template: An electron (rest mass \\(m_e = 9.11 \\times 10^{-31}\\) kg) is accelerated to \\({{beta}}c\\). (a) Calculate the Lorentz factor \\(\\gamma\\). (b) Calculate the total energy of the electron. (c = 3 × 10\\(^8\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(\\gamma = \\frac{1}{\\sqrt{1-{{beta}}^2}}\\) [1 mark]. (b) \\(E = \\gamma m_e c^2\\) [2 marks]. Substitute values to get numerical answer in J [1 mark].

subject: physics
title: Specific Heat Capacity
stem_template: A mass of \\({{m}}\\) kg of water is heated from \\({{T1}}\\) °C to \\({{T2}}\\) °C. Calculate the energy required. (Specific heat capacity of water = 4200 J kg\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))

subject: physics
title: Latent Heat of Vaporization
stem_template: Calculate the energy needed to vaporize \\({{m}}\\) kg of water at 100 °C. (Specific latent heat of vaporization of water = 2.26 × 10\\(^6\\) J kg\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: Use Q = mL for phase changes
No temperature change occurs during a phase change

subject: physics
title: Thermal Radiation and Stefan–Boltzmann Law
stem_template: A black body of surface area \\({{A}}\\) m\\(^2\\) is at temperature \\({{T}}\\) K. What is the power radiated? (Stefan–Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10\\(^{-8}\\) W m\\(^{-2}\\) K\\(^{-4}\\))

subject: physics
title: Heat Transfer by Conduction
stem_template: A glass window pane has area \\({{A}}\\) m\\(^2\\), thickness \\({{d}}\\) mm, and thermal conductivity 0.8 W m\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\). The temperature difference across it is \\({{deltaT}}\\) °C. Calculate the rate of heat transfer.

subject: physics
title: Albedo and Absorbed Radiation
stem_template: Earth receives solar intensity \\({{S}}\\) W m\\(^{-2}\\) and has an average albedo of \\({{alpha}}\\). What fraction of incoming solar radiation is absorbed by Earth?
hints: Albedo is the fraction of radiation reflected, not absorbed

subject: physics
title: Effective Temperature of Earth
stem_template: Using the Stefan-Boltzmann law and energy balance, estimate Earth's effective temperature given: Solar constant S = 1361 W m\\(^{-2}\\), albedo α = \\({{alpha}}\\), σ = 5.67 × 10\\(^{-8}\\) W m\\(^{-2}\\) K\\(^{-4}\\). Use the formula \\(T = \\left(\\frac{S(1-\\alpha)}{4\\sigma}\\right)^{1/4}\\).
hints: Set them equal and solve for T

subject: physics
title: Greenhouse Effect and Enhanced Warming
stem_template: Explain, using an energy balance diagram, how the greenhouse effect causes Earth's surface temperature to be higher than its effective blackbody temperature. State TWO greenhouse gases and describe their molecular mechanism for absorbing infrared radiation.
mark_scheme_template: Energy balance: solar in = IR out at TOA [1]. Greenhouse gases (e.g. CO₂, H₂O, CH₄) absorb outgoing IR and re-emit in all directions [1]. Net effect is reduced outgoing radiation, increasing surface temperature [1]. Molecular mechanism: vibrational modes of polyatomic molecules match IR frequencies [1]. [4 marks]
hints: Consider the energy balance at the top of atmosphere
Greenhouse gases are polyatomic molecules with IR-active vibrational modes

subject: physics
title: Ideal Gas Law: Find Pressure
stem_template: A sample of \\({{n}}\\) mol of ideal gas occupies \\({{V}}\\) L at temperature \\({{T}}\\) °C. Calculate the pressure. (R = 8.31 J mol\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: T must be in Kelvin: T(K) = T(°C) + 273
V must be in m³: V(m³) = V(L) × 10⁻³

subject: physics
title: Gas Law: Combined Changes
stem_template: A gas occupies \\({{V1}}\\) L at pressure \\({{P1}}\\) kPa and temperature \\({{T1}}\\) °C. It is heated to \\({{T2}}\\) °C at constant volume. Find the new pressure.

subject: physics
title: Root Mean Square Speed
stem_template: What is the root mean square speed of nitrogen molecules (molar mass 28 g mol\\(^{-1}\\)) at \\({{T}}\\) °C? (R = 8.31 J mol\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: M is molar mass in kg/mol

subject: physics
title: First Law of Thermodynamics
stem_template: A gas absorbs \\({{Q}}\\) J of heat and does \\({{W}}\\) J of work on its surroundings. Calculate the change in internal energy of the gas. State whether the internal energy increases or decreases.
mark_scheme_template: \\(\\Delta U = Q - W = {{Q}} - {{W}} = {{Q-W}}\\) J [2 marks]. Since \\(\\Delta U {{'>0' if Q>W else '<0'}}\\), internal energy {{increases if Q>W else decreases}}. [1 mark]
hints: Q is heat INTO the system, W is work done BY the system

subject: physics
title: Carnot Efficiency
stem_template: A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at \\({{Th}}\\) °C and a cold reservoir at \\({{Tc}}\\) °C. Calculate its maximum efficiency.
hints: Both temperatures must be in Kelvin

subject: physics
title: Entropy Change
stem_template: \\({{Q}}\\) J of heat flows reversibly into a gas at constant temperature \\({{T}}\\) K. What is the entropy change of the gas?
hints: ΔS = Q/T for reversible process at constant T

subject: physics
title: Ohm's Law: Find Current
stem_template: A resistor of \\({{R}}\\) Ω is connected to a \\({{V}}\\) V battery. Calculate the current flowing through it.
hints: Use Ohm's law: V = IR, so I = V/R

subject: physics
title: Power Dissipated in a Resistor
stem_template: A current of \\({{I}}\\) A flows through a resistor of \\({{R}}\\) Ω. Calculate the power dissipated.
hints: P = I²R or P = V²/R or P = IV

subject: physics
title: Series-Parallel Circuit Analysis
stem_template: A circuit has a \\({{R1}}\\) Ω resistor in series with a parallel combination of \\({{R2}}\\) Ω and \\({{R3}}\\) Ω. The supply voltage is \\({{V}}\\) V. Find: (a) total resistance, (b) total current from the supply, (c) voltage across the parallel combination.
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(R_{parallel} = \\frac{{{R2}} \\times {{R3}}}{{{R2}}+{{R3}}}\\) Ω; \\(R_{total} = {{R1}} + R_{parallel}\\) Ω [2 marks]. (b) \\(I = \\frac{{{V}}}{R_{total}}\\) A [1 mark]. (c) \\(V_{parallel} = I \\times R_{parallel}\\) V [1 mark].
hints: Find equivalent resistance of parallel branch first
Use V = IR for each component

subject: physics
title: Internal Resistance
stem_template: A battery with EMF \\({{emf}}\\) V and internal resistance \\({{r}}\\) Ω is connected to an external resistance \\({{R}}\\) Ω. What is the terminal voltage?
mark_scheme_template: Current: \\(I = \\frac{\\varepsilon}{R+r} = \\frac{{{emf}}}{{{R}}+{{r}}}\\). Terminal voltage: \\(V = IR = {{emf*R/(R+r)}}\\) V [2 marks]
hints: Terminal voltage V = ε − Ir = IR

subject: physics
title: Period of a Simple Pendulum
stem_template: Calculate the period of a simple pendulum of length \\({{L}}\\) m. (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\))

subject: physics
title: Spring-Mass Period
stem_template: A mass of \\({{m}}\\) kg is attached to a spring with spring constant \\({{k}}\\) N m\\(^{-1}\\). Calculate the period of oscillation.

subject: physics
title: Energy in SHM
stem_template: A mass-spring system oscillates with amplitude \\({{A}}\\) m and spring constant \\({{k}}\\) N m\\(^{-1}\\). What is the maximum kinetic energy?
mark_scheme_template: Maximum KE = maximum PE = \\(\\frac{1}{2}kA^2 = \\frac{1}{2} \\times {{k}} \\times {{A}}^2 = {{0.5*k*A*A}}\\) J [2 marks]
hints: Maximum KE occurs at equilibrium position

subject: physics
title: SHM Equations of Motion
stem_template: A particle undergoes SHM with amplitude \\({{A}}\\) cm and period \\({{T}}\\) s. The displacement is given by \\(x = A\\cos(\\omega t)\\). (a) Find ω. (b) Find the maximum velocity. (c) Find the acceleration when x = \\({{x0}}\\) cm.

subject: physics
title: Wave Speed
stem_template: A wave has frequency \\({{f}}\\) Hz and wavelength \\({{lam}}\\) m. Calculate the wave speed.

subject: physics
title: Transverse vs Longitudinal Waves
stem_template: Which of the following is an example of a longitudinal wave?
mark_scheme_template: Sound waves are longitudinal: particles vibrate parallel to the direction of energy transfer. All other options are transverse waves. [1 mark]
hints: In longitudinal waves, particle oscillation is parallel to wave propagation

subject: physics
title: Wave Properties from Graph
stem_template: A transverse wave is described by \\(y = {{A}} \\times 10^{-2} \\sin({{k}}x - {{omega}}t)\\) where x and y are in metres and t in seconds. State: (a) amplitude (b) wavelength (c) frequency (d) wave speed.
hints: Compare with y = A sin(kx − ωt)

subject: physics
title: Snell's Law of Refraction
stem_template: Light travels from air (n = 1.00) into glass (n = \\({{n}}\\)). The angle of incidence is \\({{theta_i}}°\\). What is the angle of refraction?
hints: Light bends towards normal when entering denser medium

subject: physics
title: Young's Double Slit: Fringe Spacing
stem_template: In a Young's double slit experiment, the slit separation is \\({{d}}\\) mm, and the screen is \\({{L}}\\) m away. The light has wavelength \\({{lam}}\\) nm. Calculate the fringe spacing.
hints: Convert all to SI units: λ in m, d in m

subject: physics
title: Single Slit Diffraction Minimum
stem_template: Light of wavelength \\({{lam}}\\) nm passes through a single slit of width \\({{b}}\\) μm. Calculate the angle of the first diffraction minimum.
hints: First minimum condition: b sinθ = λ

subject: physics
title: Standing Wave on a String: Harmonics
stem_template: A string of length \\({{L}}\\) m has a wave speed of \\({{v}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\). Calculate the frequency of the \\({{n}}\\)th harmonic.
mark_scheme_template: For the nth harmonic of a string fixed at both ends: \\(f_n = \\frac{nv}{2L} = \\frac{{{n}} \\times {{v}}}{2 \\times {{L}}} = {{n*v/(2*L)}}\\) Hz [2 marks]
hints: For string fixed at both ends: fn = nv/2L

subject: physics
title: Open Pipe Resonance
stem_template: An open pipe of length \\({{L}}\\) m has speed of sound \\({{v}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\). Which of the following is a resonant frequency?
mark_scheme_template: Open pipe: both ends are antinodes. Fundamental frequency: \\(f_1 = \\frac{v}{2L} = \\frac{{{v}}}{2 \\times {{L}}} = {{v/(2*L)}}\\) Hz [1 mark]

subject: physics
title: Resonance in Closed Pipe
stem_template: A closed pipe resonates at its fundamental frequency with a tuning fork of \\({{f}}\\) Hz. (a) Calculate the length of the pipe. (b) Find the next resonant frequency. (Speed of sound = 340 m s\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: (a) Closed pipe fundamental: \\(f_1 = v/(4L)\\), so \\(L = v/(4f) = 340/(4 \\times {{f}}) = {{340/(4*f)}}\\) m [2 marks]. (b) Closed pipe supports odd harmonics only: next is 3rd harmonic, \\(f_3 = 3 \\times {{f}} = {{3*f}}\\) Hz [1 mark].
hints: Closed pipe has a node at closed end and antinode at open end
Closed pipe supports only odd harmonics: f, 3f, 5f...

subject: physics
title: Doppler Effect: Moving Source
stem_template: A siren emits sound at \\({{f0}}\\) Hz. It moves towards a stationary observer at \\({{vs}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\). Calculate the observed frequency. (Speed of sound = 340 m s\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: f_obs = f₀ × v/(v − v_s) when source moves towards observer
Observed frequency is higher when source approaches

subject: physics
title: Doppler Effect: Receding Source
stem_template: A star moves away from Earth at \\({{v}}\\) × 10\\(^6\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\). It emits light at \\({{lambda0}}\\) nm. What is the observed wavelength? (c = 3 × 10\\(^8\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\))
hints: Use Δλ/λ₀ ≈ v/c for non-relativistic speeds

subject: physics
title: Doppler Application: Speed Measurement
stem_template: A radar gun emits microwaves at \\({{f0}}\\) × 10\\(^9\\) Hz. A car moving towards the gun at \\({{v}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) reflects the waves back. Calculate the beat frequency between transmitted and received waves. (c = 3 × 10\\(^8\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: Beat frequency ≈ \\(\\frac{2v f_0}{c} = \\frac{2 \\times {{v}} \\times {{f0}} \\times 10^9}{3 \\times 10^8} = {{2*f0*1e9*v/3e8}}\\) Hz [3 marks]. Double Doppler shift because car acts as both receiver and reflector.
hints: Double Doppler effect: car receives shifted frequency then re-emits it

subject: physics
title: Gravitational Field Strength
stem_template: Calculate the gravitational field strength at the surface of a planet of mass \\({{M}}\\) × 10\\(^{24}\\) kg and radius \\({{R}}\\) × 10\\(^6\\) m. (G = 6.67 × 10\\(^{-11}\\) N m\\(^2\\) kg\\(^{-2}\\))

subject: physics
title: Orbital Period (Kepler's Third Law)
stem_template: A satellite orbits Earth at radius \\({{r}}\\) × 10\\(^6\\) m. Calculate its orbital period. (G = 6.67 × 10\\(^{-11}\\) N m\\(^2\\) kg\\(^{-2}\\), M_Earth = 6.0 × 10\\(^{24}\\) kg)

subject: physics
title: Gravitational Potential Energy
stem_template: Two masses \\(m_1\\) = \\({{m1}}\\) kg and \\(m_2\\) = \\({{m2}}\\) kg are separated by \\({{r}}\\) m. What is the gravitational potential energy of the system? (G = 6.67 × 10\\(^{-11}\\) N m\\(^2\\) kg\\(^{-2}\\))
mark_scheme_template: \\(E_p = -\\frac{Gm_1m_2}{r} = -\\frac{6.67 \\times 10^{-11} \\times {{m1}} \\times {{m2}}}{{{r}}} = {{-6.67e-11*m1*m2/r}}\\) J [2 marks]. Gravitational PE is always negative.
hints: The negative sign indicates a bound system

subject: physics
title: Coulomb's Law: Electric Force
stem_template: Two point charges of \\({{q1}}\\) μC and \\({{q2}}\\) μC are separated by \\({{r}}\\) cm. Calculate the electrostatic force between them. (k = 8.99 × 10\\(^9\\) N m\\(^2\\) C\\(^{-2}\\))
hints: Convert μC to C (× 10⁻⁶) and cm to m (× 10⁻²)

subject: physics
title: Magnetic Force on a Current
stem_template: A wire of length \\({{L}}\\) m carries current \\({{I}}\\) A perpendicular to a magnetic field of \\({{B}}\\) T. Calculate the magnetic force on the wire.
hints: F = BIL (for wire perpendicular to field)
Use F = BIL sinθ in general; here θ = 90°

subject: physics
title: Electric Field Strength
stem_template: A point charge of \\({{q}}\\) μC creates an electric field at a distance of \\({{r}}\\) m. What is the field strength? (k = 8.99 × 10\\(^9\\) N m\\(^2\\) C\\(^{-2}\\))
hints: E = kq/r² for a point charge

subject: physics
title: Circular Motion in Magnetic Field
stem_template: A proton (mass 1.67 × 10\\(^{-27}\\) kg, charge 1.6 × 10\\(^{-19}\\) C) moves at \\({{v}}\\) × 10\\(^6\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of \\({{B}}\\) T. (a) Find the radius of its circular path. (b) Find the period of revolution.
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(qvB = \\frac{mv^2}{r}\\), so \\(r = \\frac{mv}{qB} = \\frac{1.67 \\times 10^{-27} \\times {{v}} \\times 10^6}{1.6 \\times 10^{-19} \\times {{B}}}\\) m [2 marks]. (b) \\(T = \\frac{2\\pi r}{v} = \\frac{2\\pi m}{qB}\\) s (independent of v) [2 marks].
hints: Lorentz force = centripetal force: qvB = mv²/r

subject: physics
title: Velocity Selector
stem_template: In a velocity selector, electric field \\(E\\) = \\({{E}}\\) kV m\\(^{-1}\\) and magnetic field \\(B\\) = \\({{B}}\\) T are perpendicular. What speed of particle passes through undeflected?
mark_scheme_template: Undeflected when electric force = magnetic force: \\(qE = qvB\\), so \\(v = E/B = {{E*1000}}/{{B}} = {{round(E*1000/B,0)}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) [2 marks]
hints: Electric force qE must balance magnetic force qvB

subject: physics
title: Faraday's Law: Induced EMF
stem_template: A coil of \\({{N}}\\) turns has its magnetic flux changed from \\({{phi1}}\\) mWb to \\({{phi2}}\\) mWb in \\({{t}}\\) ms. Calculate the magnitude of the induced EMF.
hints: Convert mWb to Wb and ms to s

subject: physics
title: Lenz's Law Direction
stem_template: A bar magnet's north pole is thrust into a coil. According to Lenz's law, the induced current in the coil will:
answer_template: Create a magnetic field opposing the approaching north pole
distractor_templates: Create a magnetic field assisting the approaching north pole
Flow in the same direction as the magnet's motion
Not flow, as Lenz's law is about voltage not current

options_template.text: Create a magnetic field opposing the approaching north pole (north pole facing the magnet)
Create a magnetic field assisting the approaching north pole (south pole facing the magnet)
Flow in a direction determined only by the resistance of the coil
Not flow, as the coil is stationary

mark_scheme_template: Lenz's law: induced current opposes the change causing it. The increasing flux through the coil induces a current creating a north pole facing the approaching magnet, repelling it. [1 mark]
hints: Lenz's law is the negative sign in Faraday's law
The induced effect always opposes the change

subject: physics
title: Transformer Turns Ratio
stem_template: A transformer has \\({{Np}}\\) primary turns and \\({{Ns}}\\) secondary turns. The primary voltage is \\({{Vp}}\\) V. (a) Calculate the secondary voltage. (b) If the secondary current is \\({{Is}}\\) A, find the primary current (assume 100% efficiency).
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(V_s = \\frac{N_s}{N_p} V_p = \\frac{{{Ns}}}{{{Np}}} \\times {{Vp}} = {{round(Ns/Np*Vp, 1)}}\\) V [2]. (b) \\(P_p = P_s\\): \\(I_p V_p = I_s V_s\\), so \\(I_p = \\frac{I_s V_s}{V_p} = {{round(Is*Ns/Np, 3)}}\\) A [2].
hints: For ideal transformer: input power = output power

subject: physics
title: Energy Level Transitions
stem_template: An electron in a hydrogen atom falls from energy level \\(n = {{n2}}\\) to \\(n = {{n1}}\\). The energy levels are given by \\(E_n = -13.6/n^2\\) eV. Calculate the energy of the emitted photon.
hints: Energy of photon = energy difference between levels

subject: physics
title: Atomic Models and Spectral Lines
stem_template: Describe the Bohr model of the hydrogen atom. Explain why it predicts discrete spectral lines and calculate the wavelength of light emitted when hydrogen transitions from \\(n = {{n2}}\\) to \\(n = 2\\). (h = 6.63 × 10\\(^{-34}\\) J s, c = 3 × 10\\(^8\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\))
mark_scheme_template: Bohr model: electrons in fixed circular orbits [1]. Electrons can only jump between discrete energy levels [1]. \\(\\Delta E = 13.6(1/4 - 1/{{n2}}^2) = {{round(13.6*(1/4-1/n2**2),3)}}\\) eV [1]. \\(\\lambda = hc/\\Delta E = {{round(6.63e-34*3e8/(13.6*(1/4-1/n2**2)*1.6e-19)*1e9,0)}}\\) nm [1].

subject: physics
title: Photoelectric Effect: Maximum KE
stem_template: Light of frequency \\({{f}}\\) × 10\\(^{14}\\) Hz shines on a metal with work function \\({{phi}}\\) eV. Calculate the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons. (h = 6.63 × 10\\(^{-34}\\) J s, 1 eV = 1.6 × 10\\(^{-19}\\) J)

subject: physics
title: De Broglie Wavelength
stem_template: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron (mass 9.11 × 10\\(^{-31}\\) kg) moving at \\({{v}}\\) × 10\\(^6\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\). (h = 6.63 × 10\\(^{-34}\\) J s)

subject: physics
title: Photon Energy and Momentum
stem_template: A photon has wavelength \\({{lam}}\\) nm. What is its momentum? (h = 6.63 × 10\\(^{-34}\\) J s)

subject: physics
title: Radioactive Half-Life: Remaining Activity
stem_template: A radioactive sample has initial activity \\({{A0}}\\) × 10\\(^4\\) Bq. Its half-life is \\({{t_half}}\\) hours. What is the activity after \\({{t}}\\) hours?
mark_scheme_template: Number of half-lives = \\({{t}}/{{t_half}}\\). \\(A = A_0 \\times (0.5)^{t/t_{1/2}} = {{A0}} \\times 10^4 \\times 0.5^{{{t}}/{{t_half}}} = {{round(A0*1e4*0.5**(t/t_half), 0)}}\\) Bq [3 marks]
hints: First find how many half-lives have passed: n = t/t½

subject: physics
title: Alpha Decay Equation
stem_template: Uranium-238 (\\(^{238}_{92}\\)U) undergoes alpha decay. What is the daughter nucleus?
hints: Alpha particle is ⁴₂He: loses 4 from mass number, 2 from atomic number
Conservation of mass number and atomic number

subject: physics
title: Radioactive Decay Constant
stem_template: A radioactive isotope has half-life \\({{t_half}}\\) days. (a) Calculate the decay constant in s\\(^{-1}\\). (b) A sample initially contains \\({{N0}}\\) × 10\\(^{20}\\) atoms. Find the initial activity in Bq.
answer_template: (a) λ = ln2/(t½ in s) (b) A₀ = λN₀

subject: physics
title: Mass Defect and Binding Energy
stem_template: The mass of a helium-4 nucleus is 4.0015 u. Given that proton mass = 1.00728 u and neutron mass = 1.00867 u, calculate the mass defect and the binding energy per nucleon. (1 u = 931.5 MeV)
hints: Mass defect = Δm = mass of protons + mass of neutrons − actual nuclear mass
Binding energy = Δm × 931.5 MeV per u

subject: physics
title: Nuclear Fission Energy
stem_template: In a fission reaction, \\(^{235}\\)U absorbs a neutron and splits releasing a mass deficit of \\({{dm}}\\) u per reaction. How much energy is released per reaction? (1 u = 931.5 MeV)
hints: 1 u corresponds to 931.5 MeV of energy

subject: physics
title: Nuclear Reactor: Chain Reaction and Criticality
stem_template: Describe the conditions necessary for a sustained chain reaction in a nuclear reactor. Explain the role of: (a) moderator, (b) control rods, (c) coolant.
answer_template: Qualitative description of critical mass, moderation, and control.
mark_scheme_template: (a) Moderator (e.g., graphite, water) slows fast neutrons to thermal speeds, increasing probability of fission [2]. (b) Control rods (e.g., boron) absorb neutrons to control reaction rate; inserted to slow/stop, withdrawn to increase rate [2]. (c) Coolant transfers heat from core to generate steam for electricity [1]. Sustained chain reaction requires multiplication factor k ≥ 1 [1]. [6 marks]
hints: Fast neutrons are less likely to cause fission in ²³⁵U

subject: physics
title: Fusion Reaction: Energy Release
stem_template: The proton-proton chain results in the fusion of four protons into one helium-4 nucleus. Given the masses: proton = 1.00728 u, \\(^4\\)He = 4.00260 u, and 2 positrons (each 0.000549 u) are released. Calculate the total energy released per reaction. (1 u = 931.5 MeV)
mark_scheme_template: Mass of reactants = 4 × 1.00728 = 4.02912 u [1]. Mass of products = 4.00260 + 2 × 0.000549 = 4.00370 u [1]. Δm = 4.02912 − 4.00370 = 0.02542 u [1]. E = 0.02542 × 931.5 ≈ 23.7 MeV [1]. (Plus annihilation: 2 × 2 × 0.511 MeV additional energy from positron-electron annihilation.)
hints: Positrons will annihilate with electrons releasing additional 2 × 0.511 MeV each

subject: physics
title: HR Diagram: Stellar Evolution
stem_template: On a Hertzsprung-Russell (HR) diagram, where are main sequence stars located?
answer_template: Along a diagonal band from upper-left (hot, bright) to lower-right (cool, dim)

options_template.text: Along a diagonal band from upper-left (hot, luminous) to lower-right (cool, dim)
In the upper-right corner (cool, very luminous — red giants)
In the lower-left corner (hot, dim — white dwarfs)

mark_scheme_template: Main sequence is a diagonal band from upper-left (O-type, hot, blue, luminous) to lower-right (M-type, cool, red, dim). Stars fuse hydrogen in their cores on the main sequence. [1 mark]

subject: physics
title: Stellar Luminosity: Stefan–Boltzmann Law
stem_template: A star has surface temperature \\({{T}}\\) × 10\\(^3\\) K and radius \\({{R}}\\) × 10\\(^8\\) m. Calculate its luminosity. (σ = 5.67 × 10\\(^{-8}\\) W m\\(^{-2}\\) K\\(^{-4}\\))
hints: Treat star as a perfect blackbody

subject: physics
title: Nucleosynthesis and Stellar Life Cycles
stem_template: Describe the sequence of nuclear reactions in a star like the Sun. Explain what happens after the main sequence phase, referring to the HR diagram.
answer_template: Qualitative description: main sequence H fusion → red giant → He fusion → planetary nebula → white dwarf
mark_scheme_template: Main sequence: H → He fusion (p-p chain) [1]. As H runs out, core contracts, outer layers expand → red giant [1]. Red giant: He → C fusion (triple-alpha process) [1]. Shell hydrogen burning continues [1]. For Sun-like star: outer layers shed → planetary nebula; core remains as white dwarf [1]. Higher-mass stars: continue to fuse up to Fe; Fe has maximum binding energy per nucleon, so fusion becomes endothermic → supernova → neutron star or black hole [1]. [6 marks]
hints: Fusion proceeds to heavier elements as each stage ends
Maximum binding energy per nucleon is at iron (Fe-56)

subject: physics
title: SUVAT: Displacement with Initial Velocity
stem_template: An object with initial velocity \\({{u}}\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) accelerates at \\({{a}}\\) m s\\(^{-2}\\) for \\({{t}}\\) s. Calculate the displacement.

subject: physics
title: Weight and Normal Force on Incline
stem_template: A block of mass \\({{m}}\\) kg rests on a smooth incline at \\({{theta}}°\\) to the horizontal. (g = 9.8 m s\\(^{-2}\\)) Calculate: (a) the normal contact force, (b) the acceleration along the incline.
answer_template: (a) N = mg cosθ (b) a = g sinθ
hints: Resolve weight parallel and perpendicular to slope
N balances perpendicular component of weight

subject: physics
title: Efficiency of a Machine
stem_template: A machine has input power \\({{Pin}}\\) W and useful output power \\({{Pout}}\\) W. Calculate the efficiency as a percentage.

subject: physics
title: Mechanisms of Heat Transfer
stem_template: Which mode of heat transfer can occur through a vacuum?
mark_scheme_template: Radiation: energy transferred by electromagnetic waves (infrared), needs no medium. Conduction and convection require a material medium. [1 mark]
hints: The Sun heats Earth through empty space — that is radiation

subject: physics
title: Which Gas Is NOT a Greenhouse Gas?
stem_template: Which of the following is NOT a greenhouse gas?
mark_scheme_template: N₂ is homonuclear diatomic: its only vibration has no change in dipole moment, so it cannot absorb infrared radiation. All other options are polyatomic with IR-active vibrational modes. [1 mark]
hints: Greenhouse gases must have IR-active vibrational modes (change in dipole moment)

subject: physics
title: Kinetic Theory: Pressure and Molecular Speed
stem_template: Using kinetic theory, show that \\(p = \\frac{1}{3}\\rho \\langle v^2 \\rangle\\). State all assumptions of the kinetic model of an ideal gas.
answer_template: Derivation using change of momentum and random motion
mark_scheme_template: Assumptions: large N, elastic collisions, random motion, no intermolecular forces, particle volume negligible [2]. Force from one particle on wall = rate of change of momentum [1]. Summing over all N particles and averaging over 3 dimensions: p = Nm\\langle v^2\\rangle/(3V) = rho\\langle v^2\\rangle/3 [2]. [5 marks]
hints: Average of vx^2 = vy^2 = vz^2 = v^2/3

subject: physics
title: Isothermal Expansion: Work and Heat
stem_template: A gas expands isothermally at \\({{T}}\\) K from \\({{V1}}\\) L to \\({{V2}}\\) L (n = \\({{n}}\\) mol). Calculate (a) work done by gas and (b) heat absorbed. (R = 8.31 J mol\\(^{-1}\\) K\\(^{-1}\\))
answer_template: W = nRT ln(V2/V1); Q = W (isothermal: deltaU=0)
mark_scheme_template: (a) \\(W = nRT\\ln(V_2/V_1) = {{n}} \\times 8.31 \\times {{T}} \\times \\ln({{V2}}/{{V1}})\\) J [3]. (b) Isothermal: \\(\\Delta U = 0\\). By first law: Q = W (heat absorbed from surroundings) [2]. [5 marks]
hints: Isothermal: T constant → deltaU = 0 → Q = W

subject: physics
title: Condition for SHM
stem_template: Which condition is necessary for simple harmonic motion?
answer_template: Restoring force proportional to and directed opposite to displacement

options_template.text: Restoring force proportional to displacement, directed towards equilibrium
Constant force always acting in the same direction

mark_scheme_template: SHM: a = -omega^2 x, meaning acceleration is proportional to and directed opposite to displacement. Equivalent to F = -kx. [1 mark]
hints: a = -omega^2 x is the defining equation of SHM

subject: physics
title: Intensity and Amplitude Relationship
stem_template: If the amplitude of a wave is doubled, by what factor does the intensity change?
mark_scheme_template: I is proportional to A^2. If A doubles: I multiplied by (2)^2 = 4. [1 mark]

subject: physics
title: Critical Angle for Total Internal Reflection
stem_template: Glass has refractive index \\({{n}}\\). Calculate the critical angle for total internal reflection.
mark_scheme_template: At critical angle: n sin(theta_c) = 1 x sin(90) = 1. So theta_c = arcsin(1/n). [2 marks]

subject: physics
title: Escape Velocity Derivation and Calculation
stem_template: Derive and calculate the escape velocity from a planet of mass \\({{M}}\\) x 10\\(^{24}\\) kg and radius \\({{R}}\\) x 10\\(^6\\) m. (G = 6.67 x 10\\(^{-11}\\) N m\\(^2\\) kg\\(^{-2}\\))
mark_scheme_template: Set KE = GPE at surface: (1/2)mv^2 = GMm/R. Solve: v_esc = sqrt(2GM/R). Substitute values numerically. [4 marks]
hints: At escape speed: KE gained = gravitational PE lost

subject: physics
title: Lorentz Force on Moving Charge
stem_template: A proton (charge 1.6 x 10\\(^{-19}\\) C) moves at \\({{v}}\\) x 10\\(^6\\) m s\\(^{-1}\\) perpendicular to a magnetic field of \\({{B}}\\) T. (a) Find the magnetic force. (b) Describe the subsequent motion.
mark_scheme_template: (a) F = qvB = 1.6e-19 x {{v}}e6 x {{B}} N [2]. (b) Force always perpendicular to velocity: uniform circular motion [2]. [4 marks]
hints: F = qvB for charge perpendicular to B
Perpendicular force: constant speed, circular path

subject: physics
title: Radius of Electron Path After Acceleration
stem_template: An electron (m = 9.11 x 10\\(^{-31}\\) kg, e = 1.6 x 10\\(^{-19}\\) C) is accelerated through \\({{V}}\\) V then enters a field B = \\({{B}}\\) T perpendicular to velocity. Find the radius of its circular path.
mark_scheme_template: KE from acceleration: eV = (1/2)mv^2, so v = sqrt(2eV/m) [1]. Radius: r = mv/(eB) [1]. Combined: r = sqrt(2mV/e)/B [2]. Substitute numerical values. [4 marks]

subject: physics
title: Peak EMF of AC Generator
stem_template: A coil with \\({{N}}\\) turns, area \\({{A}}\\) cm\\(^2\\), rotates at \\({{f}}\\) Hz in a uniform field of \\({{B}}\\) T. Calculate the peak EMF.
mark_scheme_template: EMF_0 = NBAomega = N x B x A(m^2) x 2pi x f. Convert A from cm^2: x10^-4. Calculate numerical result. [3 marks]
hints: Convert A: cm^2 to m^2 by x 10^-4

subject: physics
title: Rutherford Scattering Experiment
stem_template: In Rutherford's gold foil experiment, a small fraction of alpha particles were deflected at large angles. What does this indicate?
answer_template: The atom contains a small, dense, positively charged nucleus
distractor_templates: Electrons are distributed throughout the atom
The positive charge is spread uniformly

options_template.text: The atom has a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus that strongly deflects nearby alpha particles
Electrons are distributed throughout the atom in a diffuse cloud
The positive charge is spread uniformly (Thomson plum-pudding model)
Alpha particles have no charge and deflect randomly

mark_scheme_template: Rare large-angle deflections show: concentrated positive charge in a tiny nucleus. Most particles pass straight through (empty space). Led to nuclear model. [2 marks]
hints: Rare deflections back: small, massive, positive nucleus

subject: physics
title: Threshold Frequency and Stopping Potential
stem_template: A metal has work function \\({{phi}}\\) eV. (a) Find the threshold frequency. (b) Find the stopping potential when light of \\({{f}}\\) x 10\\(^{14}\\) Hz shines on it. (h = 6.63 x 10\\(^{-34}\\) J s, e = 1.6 x 10\\(^{-19}\\) C)
hints: Stopping potential: eVs = KE_max = hf - phi

subject: physics
title: Beta-Minus Decay Equation
stem_template: Carbon-14 undergoes beta-minus decay. Which equation correctly represents this?
mark_scheme_template: Beta-minus: neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino. Z: 6→7 (N formed). A unchanged: 14. [1 mark]
hints: Beta-minus: Z increases by 1, A unchanged
Antineutrino is emitted in beta-minus decay

subject: physics
title: Binding Energy Curve Implications
stem_template: The binding energy per nucleon peaks near iron-56. Which statement follows from this?
answer_template: Both fusion of light nuclei and fission of heavy nuclei can release energy
distractor_templates: Only fission of heavy nuclei releases energy
Only fusion of light nuclei releases energy
Iron-56 releases the most energy in any nuclear reaction

options_template.text: Both fusion (light → heavier) and fission (heavy → lighter, towards Fe-56) release energy
Only fission releases energy; fusion is endothermic
Only fusion releases energy; fission is endothermic
Iron-56 releases the most energy when it undergoes fission

mark_scheme_template: Processes moving towards max BE/nucleon (Fe-56) release energy. Fusing light nuclei (H to He) and splitting heavy nuclei (U to medium-mass) both move toward the peak → energy released. [2 marks]
hints: Higher BE/nucleon = more stable = lower energy
Moving toward Fe-56 peak always releases energy

subject: physics
title: Newton's Laws of Motion
stem_template: Which of the following statements correctly apply Newton's Second Law (\(F = ma\))? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: A larger force on the same mass produces greater acceleration
An object at rest has no forces acting on it
If net force is zero, acceleration is zero
Mass and weight are the same quantity
The unit of force (Newton) equals kg·m·s⁻²

mark_scheme_template: TRUE: F∝a for constant m; net F=0 → a=0; N=kg·m·s⁻². FALSE: object at rest has balanced forces (e.g. gravity + normal); weight=mg≠m.
hints: Objects at rest still have gravity balanced by normal force.

subject: physics
title: Wave Properties
stem_template: Which of the following correctly describe properties of transverse waves? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: Particle displacement is perpendicular to wave propagation direction
Light is an example of a transverse wave
Transverse waves require a medium to travel through
Amplitude affects the energy carried by the wave

mark_scheme_template: Transverse: perpendicular displacement; EM waves (light) are transverse; higher amplitude = more energy. FALSE: sound is longitudinal; EM waves (light) travel through vacuum — no medium needed.
hints: Light is EM wave — it can travel through vacuum.

subject: physics
title: Electrical Circuit Concepts
stem_template: Which of the following are TRUE about components connected in SERIES in a circuit? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: The same current flows through all components
The voltage is the same across all components
Total resistance = sum of individual resistances
If one component fails (open circuit), all components stop
Total voltage = sum of individual voltages across each component

mark_scheme_template: Series: same current everywhere; total R=R₁+R₂+...; total V=V₁+V₂+...; one break stops all. FALSE: voltage DIVIDES (not equal) across components in series.
hints: Series: one path for current → same current everywhere.
Compare with parallel circuits where voltage is shared equally.

subject: physics
title: Projectile Motion Step-by-Step
stem_template: A ball is launched horizontally at \({{v}}\) m/s from a height of \({{h}}\) m. Find the time to reach the ground and the horizontal range.

steps_template.label: Calculate horizontal range (m)
steps_template.content: Horizontal: \(R = v \times t = {{v}} \times t\). Give answer to 3 s.f.

hints: Horizontal and vertical motion are independent.

subject: physics
title: SUVAT Problem-Solving Order
stem_template: Arrange the following steps for solving a SUVAT kinematics problem in the CORRECT ORDER.
items: Identify which SUVAT equation to use (contains the required variable and the known ones)
List all known quantities: s, u, v, a, t
Substitute values and calculate the unknown
Read the problem and define positive direction
mark_scheme_template: Correct order: (1) read + define direction; (2) list knowns; (3) select equation; (4) substitute and solve.
hints: You need 3 knowns to find the 2 unknowns using one equation.
